verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּסְעוּ֙ מֵֽאֵילִ֔ם וַיָּבֹ֜אוּ כׇּל־עֲדַ֤ת בְּנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ אֶל־מִדְבַּר־סִ֔ין אֲשֶׁ֥ר בֵּין־אֵילִ֖ם וּבֵ֣ין סִינָ֑י בַּחֲמִשָּׁ֨ה עָשָׂ֥ר יוֹם֙ לַחֹ֣דֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִ֔י לְצֵאתָ֖ם מֵאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And they took their journey from Elim, and all the congregation of the children of Israel came unto the wilderness of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after their departing out of the land of Egypt.
verses.rendering: They set out from Elim, and the whole congregation of the sons of Israel came to the wilderness of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after they had departed from the land of Egypt.
translator_notes: The wilderness of Sin (not related to the English word 'sin') is located between Elim and Sinai. The precise date — fifteenth day of the second month — places this exactly one month after the exodus. Israel has been free for thirty days and is now hungry.

verses.text_hebrew: (וילינו) [וַיִּלּ֜וֹנוּ] כׇּל־עֲדַ֧ת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל עַל־מֹשֶׁ֥ה וְעַֽל־אַהֲרֹ֖ן בַּמִּדְבָּֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And the whole congregation of the children of Israel murmured against Moses and Aaron in the wilderness:
verses.rendering: The whole congregation of the sons of Israel grumbled against Moses and Aaron in the wilderness.
translator_notes: The second wilderness murmuring (cf. 15:24). The pattern of deliverance-then-complaint is becoming structural: God provides, Israel travels, difficulty arises, Israel complains, God provides again.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּאמְר֨וּ אֲלֵהֶ֜ם בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל מִֽי־יִתֵּ֨ן מוּתֵ֤נוּ בְיַד־יְהֹוָה֙ בְּאֶ֣רֶץ מִצְרַ֔יִם בְּשִׁבְתֵּ֙נוּ֙ עַל־סִ֣יר הַבָּשָׂ֔ר בְּאׇכְלֵ֥נוּ לֶ֖חֶם לָשֹׂ֑בַע כִּֽי־הוֹצֵאתֶ֤ם אֹתָ֙נוּ֙ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּ֣ר הַזֶּ֔ה לְהָמִ֛ית אֶת־כׇּל־הַקָּהָ֥ל הַזֶּ֖ה בָּרָעָֽב׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: And the children of Israel said unto them, Would to God we had died by the hand of the LORD in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the flesh pots, and when we did eat bread to the full; for ye have brought us forth into this wilderness, to kill this whole assembly with hunger.
verses.rendering: The sons of Israel said to them, "If only we had died by the hand of the LORD in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the meat pots, when we ate bread to satisfaction! For you have brought us out into this wilderness to kill this whole assembly with hunger."
translator_notes: The complaint idealizes Egypt: 'meat pots' and 'bread to satisfaction.' Slavery is retroactively romanticized because freedom is currently uncomfortable. The accusation that Moses brought them out 'to kill this whole assembly with hunger' inverts the reality — God brought them out to give them life.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהֹוָה֙ אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֔ה הִנְנִ֨י מַמְטִ֥יר לָכֶ֛ם לֶ֖חֶם מִן־הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם וְיָצָ֨א הָעָ֤ם וְלָֽקְטוּ֙ דְּבַר־י֣וֹם בְּיוֹמ֔וֹ לְמַ֧עַן אֲנַסֶּ֛נּוּ הֲיֵלֵ֥ךְ בְּתוֹרָתִ֖י אִם־לֹֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Then said the LORD unto Moses, Behold, I will rain bread from heaven for you; and the people shall go out and gather a certain rate every day, that I may prove them, whether they will walk in my law, or no.
verses.rendering: Then the LORD said to Moses, "I am about to rain bread from heaven for you, and the people shall go out and gather a day's portion each day, so that I may test them — whether they will walk according to My instruction or not.
translator_notes: 'Rain bread from heaven' (mamtir lakhem lechem min-hashamayim) — God's response to complaint is not rebuke but provision. The bread from heaven will become a defining image of divine sustenance. The test (nasah) embedded in the provision is whether Israel will obey the gathering rules — daily trust, no hoarding.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָיָה֙ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁ֔י וְהֵכִ֖ינוּ אֵ֣ת אֲשֶׁר־יָבִ֑יאוּ וְהָיָ֣ה מִשְׁנֶ֔ה עַ֥ל אֲשֶֽׁר־יִלְקְט֖וּ י֥וֹם ׀ יֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: And it shall come to pass, that on the sixth day they shall prepare that which they bring in; and it shall be twice as much as they gather daily.
verses.rendering: On the sixth day, when they prepare what they bring in, it shall be twice as much as they gather daily."
translator_notes: The double portion on the sixth day anticipates the Sabbath before Sinai formally legislates it. The rhythm of six-and-one is built into the manna before it is built into the law.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר מֹשֶׁה֙ וְאַהֲרֹ֔ן אֶֽל־כׇּל־בְּנֵ֖י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל עֶ֕רֶב וִֽידַעְתֶּ֕ם כִּ֧י יְהֹוָ֛ה הוֹצִ֥יא אֶתְכֶ֖ם מֵאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses and Aaron said unto all the children of Israel, At even, then ye shall know that the LORD hath brought you out from the land of Egypt:
verses.rendering: So Moses and Aaron said to all the sons of Israel, "At evening you will know that it was the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt,
translator_notes: 'You will know that it was the LORD who brought you out' — the knowledge formula returns, now directed at Israel rather than Egypt. Israel too must learn who YHWH is, and they learn through provision, not only through judgment.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבֹ֗קֶר וּרְאִיתֶם֙ אֶת־כְּב֣וֹד יְהֹוָ֔ה בְּשׇׁמְע֥וֹ אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶ֖ם עַל־יְהֹוָ֑ה וְנַ֣חְנוּ מָ֔ה כִּ֥י (תלונו) [תַלִּ֖ינוּ] עָלֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And in the morning, then ye shall see the glory of the LORD; for that he heareth your murmurings against the LORD: and what are we, that ye murmur against us?
verses.rendering: and in the morning you will see the glory of the LORD, because He has heard your grumbling against the LORD. For what are we, that you grumble against us?"
verses.expanded_rendering: The Hebrew kavod ('glory') — from the root k-v-d meaning 'heavy, weighty' — describes not a glow but a presence so real it has substance. When the glory of the LORD appears in the cloud over the wilderness, it means God's full, tangible reality becomes perceptible to human senses. Kavod is what fills a room and makes everyone fall silent. It is the weight of God showing up. This first wilderness appearance of kavod will intensify at Sinai and reach its climax when the glory fills the completed tabernacle.
translator_notes: 'The glory of the LORD' (kevod YHWH) — the first appearance of kavod in a theophany context in Exodus. God's weighty, visible presence appears in the cloud in response to Israel's grumbling. Kavod manifests not in the spectacular (as at Sinai) but in the everyday (feeding a hungry people).

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, weight, substance, visible divine presence
key_terms.note: The first appearance of kavod in a theophany context in Exodus. God's glory appears in the cloud — not as abstract brightness but as the weighty, tangible reality of divine presence. Kavod will intensify through Sinai (24:16-17) to its climax in the tabernacle (40:34-35).

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֗ה בְּתֵ֣ת יְהֹוָה֩ לָכֶ֨ם בָּעֶ֜רֶב בָּשָׂ֣ר לֶאֱכֹ֗ל וְלֶ֤חֶם בַּבֹּ֙קֶר֙ לִשְׂבֹּ֔עַ בִּשְׁמֹ֤עַ יְהֹוָה֙ אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּ֣תֵיכֶ֔ם אֲשֶׁר־אַתֶּ֥ם מַלִּינִ֖ם עָלָ֑יו וְנַ֣חְנוּ מָ֔ה לֹא־עָלֵ֥ינוּ תְלֻנֹּתֵיכֶ֖ם כִּ֥י עַל־יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses said, This shall be, when the LORD shall give you in the evening flesh to eat, and in the morning bread to the full; for that the LORD heareth your murmurings which ye murmur against him: and what are we? your murmurings are not against us, but against the LORD.
verses.rendering: Moses said, "When the LORD gives you meat to eat in the evening and bread to satisfaction in the morning — because the LORD has heard your grumbling that you grumble against Him — what are we? Your grumbling is not against us but against the LORD."
translator_notes: Moses redirects the complaint: 'Your grumbling is not against us but against the LORD.' The people think they are arguing with human leaders; Moses reveals they are arguing with God. The prophetic role includes this uncomfortable truth-telling.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר מֹשֶׁה֙ אֶֽל־אַהֲרֹ֔ן אֱמֹ֗ר אֶֽל־כׇּל־עֲדַת֙ בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל קִרְב֖וּ לִפְנֵ֣י יְהֹוָ֑ה כִּ֣י שָׁמַ֔ע אֵ֖ת תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses spake unto Aaron, Say unto all the congregation of the children of Israel, Come near before the LORD: for he hath heard your murmurings.
verses.rendering: Moses said to Aaron, "Say to the whole congregation of the sons of Israel, 'Draw near before the LORD, for He has heard your grumbling.'"
translator_notes: 'Draw near before the LORD' (qirvu lifnei YHWH) — the verb qarav ('draw near') will become the technical term for offering sacrifices (the root of qorban, 'offering'). Here it means approaching God's presence, which is about to become visible.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֗י כְּדַבֵּ֤ר אַהֲרֹן֙ אֶל־כׇּל־עֲדַ֣ת בְּנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל וַיִּפְנ֖וּ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּ֑ר וְהִנֵּה֙ כְּב֣וֹד יְהֹוָ֔ה נִרְאָ֖ה בֶּעָנָֽן׃ {פ}
verses.text_kjv: And it came to pass, as Aaron spake unto the whole congregation of the children of Israel, that they looked toward the wilderness, and, behold, the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud.
verses.rendering: As Aaron spoke to the whole congregation of the sons of Israel, they looked toward the wilderness, and the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud.
translator_notes: 'The glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud' (kevod YHWH nir'ah be'anan) — kavod becomes visible. The cloud that has been guiding them (13:21-22) now reveals the divine presence within it. The people see what they have been following.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר יְהֹוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֥ה לֵּאמֹֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.rendering: The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
translator_notes: A new divine speech introduces the specific provision: meat in the evening, bread in the morning.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁמַ֗עְתִּי אֶת־תְּלוּנֹּת֮ בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵל֒ דַּבֵּ֨ר אֲלֵהֶ֜ם לֵאמֹ֗ר בֵּ֤ין הָֽעַרְבַּ֙יִם֙ תֹּאכְל֣וּ בָשָׂ֔ר וּבַבֹּ֖קֶר תִּשְׂבְּעוּ־לָ֑חֶם וִֽידַעְתֶּ֕ם כִּ֛י אֲנִ֥י יְהֹוָ֖ה אֱלֹהֵיכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel: speak unto them, saying, At even ye shall eat flesh, and in the morning ye shall be filled with bread; and ye shall know that I am the LORD your God.
verses.rendering: "I have heard the grumbling of the sons of Israel. Speak to them, saying, 'At twilight you shall eat meat, and in the morning you shall be filled with bread. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God.'"
translator_notes: 'Then you will know that I am the LORD your God' — provision is revelation. God feeds Israel not merely to sustain them but to teach them who He is. Every meal of manna is a theology lesson.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֣י בָעֶ֔רֶב וַתַּ֣עַל הַשְּׂלָ֔ו וַתְּכַ֖ס אֶת־הַֽמַּחֲנֶ֑ה וּבַבֹּ֗קֶר הָֽיְתָה֙ שִׁכְבַ֣ת הַטַּ֔ל סָבִ֖יב לַֽמַּחֲנֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And it came to pass, that at even the quails came up, and covered the camp: and in the morning the dew lay round about the host.
verses.rendering: In the evening quail came up and covered the camp, and in the morning there was a layer of dew around the camp.
translator_notes: Quail (selav) arrive in the evening, covering the camp. These are migratory birds that cross the Sinai Peninsula and can be caught when exhausted. The natural mechanism does not diminish the divine timing.

verses.text_hebrew: וַתַּ֖עַל שִׁכְבַ֣ת הַטָּ֑ל וְהִנֵּ֞ה עַל־פְּנֵ֤י הַמִּדְבָּר֙ דַּ֣ק מְחֻסְפָּ֔ס דַּ֥ק כַּכְּפֹ֖ר עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: And when the dew that lay was gone up, behold, upon the face of the wilderness there lay a small round thing, as small as the hoar frost on the ground.
verses.rendering: When the layer of dew lifted, there on the surface of the wilderness was a fine, flaky substance, as fine as frost on the ground.
translator_notes: The manna is described precisely: fine, flaky, frost-like. The physical description grounds the miracle in sensory reality — this is something you can see, touch, and taste.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּרְא֣וּ בְנֵֽי־יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל וַיֹּ֨אמְר֜וּ אִ֤ישׁ אֶל־אָחִיו֙ מָ֣ן ה֔וּא כִּ֛י לֹ֥א יָדְע֖וּ מַה־ה֑וּא וַיֹּ֤אמֶר מֹשֶׁה֙ אֲלֵהֶ֔ם ה֣וּא הַלֶּ֔חֶם אֲשֶׁ֨ר נָתַ֧ן יְהֹוָ֛ה לָכֶ֖ם לְאׇכְלָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And when the children of Israel saw it, they said one to another, It is manna: for they wist not what it was. And Moses said unto them, This is the bread which the LORD hath given you to eat.
verses.rendering: When the sons of Israel saw it, they said to one another, "What is it?" — for they did not know what it was. Moses said to them, "It is the bread that the LORD has given you to eat.
translator_notes: 'What is it?' (man hu) — the name manna may derive from this question. Israel encounters something they have never seen before and name it by their bewilderment. The bread from heaven defies existing categories.

verses.text_hebrew: זֶ֤ה הַדָּבָר֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר צִוָּ֣ה יְהֹוָ֔ה לִקְט֣וּ מִמֶּ֔נּוּ אִ֖ישׁ לְפִ֣י אׇכְל֑וֹ עֹ֣מֶר לַגֻּלְגֹּ֗לֶת מִסְפַּר֙ נַפְשֹׁ֣תֵיכֶ֔ם אִ֛ישׁ לַאֲשֶׁ֥ר בְּאׇהֳל֖וֹ תִּקָּֽחוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded, Gather of it every man according to his eating, an omer for every man, according to the number of your persons; take ye every man for them which are in his tents.
verses.rendering: This is what the LORD has commanded: 'Each one is to gather as much as he needs. Take an omer per person, according to the number of persons each of you has in his tent.'"
translator_notes: The omer-per-person rule establishes equality: each household receives according to need, not according to power or status. The distribution principle will be cited by Paul in 2 Corinthians 8:15.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵ֖ן בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל וַֽיִּלְקְט֔וּ הַמַּרְבֶּ֖ה וְהַמַּמְעִֽיט׃
verses.text_kjv: And the children of Israel did so, and gathered, some more, some less.
verses.rendering: The sons of Israel did so. Some gathered more, some less.
translator_notes: Some gathered more, some less — human behavior varies even under divine instruction.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּמֹ֣דּוּ בָעֹ֔מֶר וְלֹ֤א הֶעְדִּיף֙ הַמַּרְבֶּ֔ה וְהַמַּמְעִ֖יט לֹ֣א הֶחְסִ֑יר אִ֥ישׁ לְפִֽי־אׇכְל֖וֹ לָקָֽטוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And when they did mete it with an omer, he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack; they gathered every man according to his eating.
verses.rendering: But when they measured it with an omer, the one who gathered much had nothing left over, and the one who gathered little had no lack. Each gathered according to what he could eat.
translator_notes: The miraculous equalization: regardless of how much each person gathered, the measure came out to exactly one omer per person. Surplus and deficit are both corrected by divine provision. This is not merely logistics but theology: God ensures sufficiency.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֥אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֖ה אֲלֵהֶ֑ם אִ֕ישׁ אַל־יוֹתֵ֥ר מִמֶּ֖נּוּ עַד־בֹּֽקֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses said, Let no man leave of it till the morning.
verses.rendering: Moses said to them, "Let no one leave any of it until morning."
translator_notes: The prohibition against keeping until morning tests daily trust. Can Israel depend on God for tomorrow's bread, or must they hoard today's?

verses.text_hebrew: וְלֹא־שָׁמְע֣וּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֗ה וַיּוֹתִ֨רוּ אֲנָשִׁ֤ים מִמֶּ֙נּוּ֙ עַד־בֹּ֔קֶר וַיָּ֥רֻם תּוֹלָעִ֖ים וַיִּבְאַ֑שׁ וַיִּקְצֹ֥ף עֲלֵהֶ֖ם מֹשֶֽׁה׃
verses.text_kjv: Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto Moses; but some of them left of it until the morning, and it bred worms, and stank: and Moses was wroth with them.
verses.rendering: But they did not listen to Moses. Some left part of it until morning, and it bred worms and stank. And Moses was angry with them.
translator_notes: Disobedience produces rot: worms and stench. The manna that sustains when gathered in trust corrupts when hoarded in anxiety. The physical consequence teaches the spiritual lesson.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּלְקְט֤וּ אֹתוֹ֙ בַּבֹּ֣קֶר בַּבֹּ֔קֶר אִ֖ישׁ כְּפִ֣י אׇכְל֑וֹ וְחַ֥ם הַשֶּׁ֖מֶשׁ וְנָמָֽס׃
verses.text_kjv: And they gathered it every morning, every man according to his eating: and when the sun waxed hot, it melted.
verses.rendering: They gathered it morning by morning, each according to what he could eat, and when the sun grew hot, it melted.
translator_notes: Daily gathering, daily dependence. The rhythm of morning collection and midday melting forces Israel into a pattern of reliance that cannot be circumvented by stockpiling.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֣י ׀ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁ֗י לָֽקְט֥וּ לֶ֙חֶם֙ מִשְׁנֶ֔ה שְׁנֵ֥י הָעֹ֖מֶר לָאֶחָ֑ד וַיָּבֹ֙אוּ֙ כׇּל־נְשִׂיאֵ֣י הָֽעֵדָ֔ה וַיַּגִּ֖ידוּ לְמֹשֶֽׁה׃
verses.text_kjv: And it came to pass, that on the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread, two omers for one man: and all the rulers of the congregation came and told Moses.
verses.rendering: On the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread — two omers per person. All the leaders of the congregation came and told Moses.
translator_notes: The double portion on the sixth day arrives without special effort — God provides the Sabbath surplus automatically. The leaders report this anomaly to Moses, not yet understanding the pattern.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֲלֵהֶ֗ם ה֚וּא אֲשֶׁ֣ר דִּבֶּ֣ר יְהֹוָ֔ה שַׁבָּת֧וֹן שַׁבַּת־קֹ֛דֶשׁ לַֽיהֹוָ֖ה מָחָ֑ר אֵ֣ת אֲשֶׁר־תֹּאפ֞וּ אֵפ֗וּ וְאֵ֤ת אֲשֶֽׁר־תְּבַשְּׁלוּ֙ בַּשֵּׁ֔לוּ וְאֵת֙ כׇּל־הָ֣עֹדֵ֔ף הַנִּ֧יחוּ לָכֶ֛ם לְמִשְׁמֶ֖רֶת עַד־הַבֹּֽקֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: And he said unto them, This is that which the LORD hath said, To morrow is the rest of the holy sabbath unto the LORD: bake that which ye will bake to day, and seethe that ye will seethe; and that which remaineth over lay up for you to be kept until the morning.
verses.rendering: He said to them, "This is what the LORD has said: 'Tomorrow is a day of solemn rest, a holy Sabbath to the LORD. Bake what you want to bake and boil what you want to boil, and set aside all that is left over to keep until morning.'"
translator_notes: 'A holy Sabbath to the LORD' (shabbat qodesh laYHWH) — the Sabbath is introduced before Sinai, before the formal commandment. The manna teaches Sabbath-keeping through practice before Sinai teaches it through law. The word shabbaton ('solemn rest') intensifies shabbat.

key_terms.rendered_as: a holy Sabbath to the LORD
key_terms.semantic_range: sabbath rest, sacred cessation, holy stopping
key_terms.note: The Sabbath appears in the wilderness before Sinai formally commands it. The manna cycle teaches Sabbath observance through lived experience before the Ten Commandments codify it in law. Sabbath is first practiced, then legislated.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיַּנִּ֤יחוּ אֹתוֹ֙ עַד־הַבֹּ֔קֶר כַּאֲשֶׁ֖ר צִוָּ֣ה מֹשֶׁ֑ה וְלֹ֣א הִבְאִ֔ישׁ וְרִמָּ֖ה לֹא־הָ֥יְתָה בּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And they laid it up till the morning, as Moses bade: and it did not stink, neither was there any worm therein.
verses.rendering: They set it aside until morning, as Moses commanded, and it did not stink, and there were no worms in it.
translator_notes: Friday's manna does not rot — the exception proves the rule. What corrupted when hoarded on other nights is preserved when kept for Sabbath. God's provision conforms to God's calendar.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֤אמֶר מֹשֶׁה֙ אִכְלֻ֣הוּ הַיּ֔וֹם כִּֽי־שַׁבָּ֥ת הַיּ֖וֹם לַיהֹוָ֑ה הַיּ֕וֹם לֹ֥א תִמְצָאֻ֖הוּ בַּשָּׂדֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses said, Eat that to day; for to day is a sabbath unto the LORD: to day ye shall not find it in the field.
verses.rendering: Moses said, "Eat it today, for today is a Sabbath to the LORD. Today you will not find it in the field.
translator_notes: Moses declares the theological principle: the Sabbath belongs to the LORD. The day is not merely a human rest period but a divine institution. No manna will appear because God Himself rests from providing — the divine Sabbath models the human one.

verses.text_hebrew: שֵׁ֥שֶׁת יָמִ֖ים תִּלְקְטֻ֑הוּ וּבַיּ֧וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִ֛י שַׁבָּ֖ת לֹ֥א יִֽהְיֶה־בּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Six days ye shall gather it; but on the seventh day, which is the sabbath, in it there shall be none.
verses.rendering: Six days you shall gather it, but on the seventh day, the Sabbath, there will be none."
translator_notes: Six days of gathering, one day of rest. The creation pattern (Genesis 1-2) is reproduced in the wilderness food cycle. Israel lives inside the rhythm God established at creation.

verses.text_hebrew: וַֽיְהִי֙ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י יָצְא֥וּ מִן־הָעָ֖ם לִלְקֹ֑ט וְלֹ֖א מָצָֽאוּ׃ {ס}
verses.text_kjv: And it came to pass, that there went out some of the people on the seventh day for to gather, and they found none.
verses.rendering: On the seventh day some of the people went out to gather, but they found none.
translator_notes: Some went out on the seventh day anyway and found nothing. Disobedience on the Sabbath is not punished with worms (as weekday hoarding was) but with absence. There is simply nothing there.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֥אמֶר יְהֹוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֑ה עַד־אָ֙נָה֙ מֵֽאַנְתֶּ֔ם לִשְׁמֹ֥ר מִצְוֺתַ֖י וְתוֹרֹתָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD said unto Moses, How long refuse ye to keep my commandments and my laws?
verses.rendering: The LORD said to Moses, "How long will you refuse to keep My commandments and My instructions?
translator_notes: God's rebuke is directed through Moses: 'How long will you refuse to keep My commandments?' The plural 'you' (teme'anu) addresses the whole people, not just the Sabbath-breakers. Corporate responsibility is assumed.

verses.text_hebrew: רְא֗וּ כִּֽי־יְהֹוָה֮ נָתַ֣ן לָכֶ֣ם הַשַּׁבָּת֒ עַל־כֵּ֠ן ה֣וּא נֹתֵ֥ן לָכֶ֛ם בַּיּ֥וֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁ֖י לֶ֣חֶם יוֹמָ֑יִם שְׁב֣וּ ׀ אִ֣ישׁ תַּחְתָּ֗יו אַל־יֵ֥צֵא אִ֛ישׁ מִמְּקֹמ֖וֹ בַּיּ֥וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: See, for that the LORD hath given you the sabbath, therefore he giveth you on the sixth day the bread of two days; abide ye every man in his place, let no man go out of his place on the seventh day.
verses.rendering: See, the LORD has given you the Sabbath; therefore on the sixth day He gives you bread for two days. Each of you stay where you are; let no one go out of his place on the seventh day."
translator_notes: 'The LORD has given you the Sabbath' (YHWH natan lakhem et-haShabbat) — the Sabbath is a gift, not merely a restriction. The double bread on the sixth day is the evidence: God provides in advance so that rest is possible. Sabbath is sustained by prior provision.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּשְׁבְּת֥וּ הָעָ֖ם בַּיּ֥וֹם הַשְּׁבִעִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: So the people rested on the seventh day.
verses.rendering: The people rested on the seventh day.
translator_notes: 'The people rested' (vayyishbetu ha'am) — the verb shavat appears for the first time in Exodus describing human rest. Israel does what God did at creation: ceases from labor on the seventh day.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּקְרְא֧וּ בֵֽית־יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל אֶת־שְׁמ֖וֹ מָ֑ן וְה֗וּא כְּזֶ֤רַע גַּד֙ לָבָ֔ן וְטַעְמ֖וֹ כְּצַפִּיחִ֥ת בִּדְבָֽשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: And the house of Israel called the name thereof Manna: and it was like coriander seed, white; and the taste of it was like wafers made with honey.
verses.rendering: The house of Israel called its name manna. It was like white coriander seed, and its taste was like wafers made with honey.
translator_notes: The name 'manna' (man) is formally given. The physical description — white coriander seed, taste of honey wafers — makes the substance vivid and appealing. Numbers 11:7-8 will give additional details.

key_terms.semantic_range: manna, what is it?, bread from heaven
key_terms.note: Named from the question 'What is it?' (man hu, v15). The bread that defies categories receives a name that preserves the mystery. Israel's daily bread in the wilderness is literally named 'What is this?'

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֣אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֗ה זֶ֤ה הַדָּבָר֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר צִוָּ֣ה יְהֹוָ֔ה מְלֹ֤א הָעֹ֙מֶר֙ מִמֶּ֔נּוּ לְמִשְׁמֶ֖רֶת לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶ֑ם לְמַ֣עַן ׀ יִרְא֣וּ אֶת־הַלֶּ֗חֶם אֲשֶׁ֨ר הֶאֱכַ֤לְתִּי אֶתְכֶם֙ בַּמִּדְבָּ֔ר בְּהוֹצִיאִ֥י אֶתְכֶ֖ם מֵאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses said, This is the thing which the LORD commandeth, Fill an omer of it to be kept for your generations; that they may see the bread wherewith I have fed you in the wilderness, when I brought you forth from the land of Egypt.
verses.rendering: Moses said, "This is what the LORD has commanded: 'Fill an omer of it to be kept throughout your generations, so that they may see the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness when I brought you out of the land of Egypt.'"
translator_notes: An omer of manna is preserved as a perpetual witness to divine provision in the wilderness. The jar will eventually be placed in the Ark of the Covenant (Hebrews 9:4), keeping the bread of the wilderness inside the holiest object in Israel.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּ֨אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֜ה אֶֽל־אַהֲרֹ֗ן קַ֚ח צִנְצֶ֣נֶת אַחַ֔ת וְתֶן־שָׁ֥מָּה מְלֹֽא־הָעֹ֖מֶר מָ֑ן וְהַנַּ֤ח אֹתוֹ֙ לִפְנֵ֣י יְהֹוָ֔ה לְמִשְׁמֶ֖רֶת לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And Moses said unto Aaron, Take a pot, and put an omer full of manna therein, and lay it up before the LORD, to be kept for your generations.
verses.rendering: Moses said to Aaron, "Take a jar and put an omer of manna in it and place it before the LORD to be kept throughout your generations."
translator_notes: Aaron is charged with the preservation — a priestly duty. The manna is placed 'before the LORD' (lifnei YHWH), meaning in God's presence, anticipating the tabernacle.

verses.text_hebrew: כַּאֲשֶׁ֛ר צִוָּ֥ה יְהֹוָ֖ה אֶל־מֹשֶׁ֑ה וַיַּנִּיחֵ֧הוּ אַהֲרֹ֛ן לִפְנֵ֥י הָעֵדֻ֖ת לְמִשְׁמָֽרֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: As the LORD commanded Moses, so Aaron laid it up before the Testimony, to be kept.
verses.rendering: As the LORD commanded Moses, Aaron placed it before the Testimony to be kept.
translator_notes: 'Before the Testimony' (lifnei ha'edut) — the Testimony refers to the tablets of the covenant that do not yet exist in the narrative. The narrator writes from the perspective of the completed tabernacle, placing the manna jar in its permanent home.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל אָֽכְל֤וּ אֶת־הַמָּן֙ אַרְבָּעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה עַד־בֹּאָ֖ם אֶל־אֶ֣רֶץ נוֹשָׁ֑בֶת אֶת־הַמָּן֙ אָֽכְל֔וּ עַד־בֹּאָ֕ם אֶל־קְצֵ֖ה אֶ֥רֶץ כְּנָֽעַן׃
verses.text_kjv: And the children of Israel did eat manna forty years, until they came to a land inhabited; they did eat manna, until they came unto the borders of the land of Canaan.
verses.rendering: The sons of Israel ate the manna forty years, until they came to an inhabited land. They ate the manna until they came to the border of the land of Canaan.
translator_notes: 'Forty years' — the manna sustained Israel for the entire wilderness period. The provision was not temporary but generational. It ceased only when Israel entered the promised land and ate its produce (Joshua 5:12).

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָעֹ֕מֶר עֲשִׂרִ֥ית הָאֵיפָ֖ה הֽוּא׃ {פ}
verses.text_kjv: Now an omer is the tenth part of an ephah.
verses.rendering: An omer is a tenth of an ephah.
translator_notes: A parenthetical measurement note: an omer equals one-tenth of an ephah (approximately 2.3 liters or 2 quarts). The narrator provides the conversion for readers unfamiliar with the measurement.
